title: I stopped using MCP for stock analysis and built Skills instead — here's why and what it produces
author: staskh1966
selftext:
**TL;DR:** MCP makes Claude fetch and assemble raw financial data — it works but hallucinates math and burns tokens. Skills offload all fetching and calculations to Python, hand Claude a clean result to narrate. One command → 150-line structured stock report, free Yahoo Finance data, zero hallucinated numbers. Open source: [trading\_skills](https://github.com/staskh/trading_skills).

# The problem with the MCP approach

Most AI trading setups wire Claude to a financial data MCP (FMP, etc.) and let it figure out the rest. You ask for a Piotroski F-Score and Claude ends up doing something like:

1. `fmp_income_statement(symbol="PFE", period="annual", limit=2)` → 200-line JSON
2. `fmp_balance_sheet_statement(...)` → another 200-line JSON
3. `fmp_cash_flow_statement(...)` → another JSON
4. Claude extracts \~12 fields across three blobs and does the arithmetic

Step 4 is where things go wrong. LLMs are bad at arithmetic. They're also bad at staying consistent when pulling the same field from two different JSON shapes. The score you get back is "probably" right.

Three other issues:

* **Locked in to vendor functions** — can't add a custom indicator without convincing the MCP maintainer
* **Token burn** — three raw API responses eat context before analysis even starts
* **No audit trail** — when the number's wrong you can't tell if it was bad data, misread JSON, or bad math

# Skills: move the computation out of Claude's head

A Skill is a prompt template + Python script. The script does all the work: fetches data, runs every calculation, and packages a compact JSON result. Claude gets the finished numbers and writes the narrative.

Same Piotroski request with a Skill:

1. You type: *"Generate a comprehensive report for PFE"*
2. Python fetches Yahoo Finance data, computes F-Score, RSI, MACD, ADX, options Greeks, everything
3. Claude gets a compact JSON and narrates it

The math runs in Python. The same way. Every time.

# Real output: PFE this morning

Here's what `report-stock` produced for Pfizer today:

**Recommendation: AVOID / WAIT**

Full breakdown:

* **Trend:** Bullish score 0.47/8 — below SMA20 and SMA50, RSI 39.3, MACD bearish, ADX 14.8 (no trend)
* **Valuation:** Fwd P/E 9.3x (attractive), Trailing P/E 19.3x
* **Dividend:** 6.55% yield — but **126.5% payout ratio**. That's not sustainable without earnings recovery
* **Piotroski F-Score: 6/9** — passed 6 of 9 criteria, computed in Python, not guessed
   * Fails: LT debt increased $4.1B YoY, current ratio declined, shares diluted
   * Passes: positive net income ($7.77B), positive ROA (3.73%), positive operating CF ($11.7B), CF > net income, gross margin improvement, higher asset turnover
* **Insider activity:** All option exercises, zero open-market buys. Neutral sentiment
* **Earnings:** 2026-05-05 BMO — 5 days out. Binary risk
* **5 option strategies** (bull call spread, bear put spread, straddle, strangle, iron condor) with max profit, max loss, and breakevens for the June expiry

Full report: [PFE\_Analysis\_Report\_2026-04-30](https://github.com/staskh/trading_skills/blob/main/examples/PFE_Analysis_Report_2026-04-30_0437.md)

All free. No FMP subscription. No hallucinated math.

# How to use it

Works natively in **Claude Code** (the CLI). If you're on **Claude Desktop**, there's a local MCP wrapper — Desktop's sandbox prevents direct local API calls so the skills are exposed as MCP tools. Same output either way.

    # Claude Code
    "Generate a comprehensive report for PFE"
    
    # Claude Desktop (via local MCP)
    same prompt, same result

Source: [github.com/staskh/trading\_skills](https://github.com/staskh/trading_skills)

*Not financial advice. Options trading involves significant risk.*

comments.author: Cute-Let-4605
comments.body: Smart approach, skills are the way, there is no magic bullet with AI.

replies.author: staskh1966
replies.body:
Indeed, so many people believe that AI is a magic bullet, but it is just a tool—smart and knowledgeable but often unpredictable. I often compare it with a good 2nd-year college student—reads a lot of smart books, always has an answer, but without self-awareness if this answer is the proper one.   
Predictable "skills" add a structure to minimise AI hallucination.  
The next step is to add a verification sub-agent—I'm working on it right now.
